title: Trinidadian curry goat
subtitle: Bone-in goat slow-braised in Trinidadian curry with fresh herbs
excerpt: Bone-in goat braised for two hours in a cumin-forward Trinidadian curry with tomato, thyme, and scotch bonnet. Richer and more herb-driven than the Jamaican version, with a slightly drier sauce.
sourceNotes: Trinidadian curry goat follows the same Indo-Trinidadian curry tradition as curry chicken, with the same green seasoning base and 'burnt curry' technique. The goat takes longer than chicken and benefits from an overnight marinade. Goat meat is rarely stocked outside specialist Caribbean and halal butchers; this recipe uses lamb neck (bone-in) as a substitute, which has comparable collagen content and benefits similarly from long, slow braising. Note: goat meat should be added to the master ingredient table when available.

content.text: Trinidadian curry goat shares the same architecture as the Jamaican version: a long, slow braise, bone-in, with lots of spice. But the character is different. Where Jamaican curry goat is allspice-forward and often finished with potato, the Trinidadian version is cumin-forward, more herb-driven from the green seasoning base, and the sauce tends to be slightly drier and more concentrated.

Lamb neck is the most faithful substitute for goat: bone-in, well-marbled, and rich in collagen, it behaves in the pot in almost the same way. An overnight marinade in green seasoning is strongly worth doing. Two hours of braising is the minimum; the meat should pull away from the bone without effort.

items.name: Lamb neck
items.prepNote: bone-in, cut into large pieces, goat meat if available from a Caribbean or halal butcher

content.text: Blend the {{spring-onion}} spring onions, {{garlic}} of garlic, {{ginger-root}} of ginger, {{coriander}} of coriander, {{thyme-fresh}} of fresh thyme, and {{scotch-bonnet}} scotch bonnet with 2 tablespoons of water to a rough paste. Rub all over the {{lamb-neck}} of lamb neck pieces. Season with {{sea-salt-fine}} of salt. Cover and refrigerate for at least 8 hours, ideally overnight.

Heat the {{vegetable-oil}} of oil in a Dutch oven over medium-high heat. Add the {{curry-powder}} of curry powder and {{cumin-seeds}} of cumin seeds to the hot oil. Toast for 30 seconds, then add the {{onion}} onion. Cook for 4 minutes. Add the marinated meat and brown on all sides, about 6 minutes. Add the {{tomato}} tomatoes and 400 ml of cold water. Bring to a boil.

Reduce to a low simmer, cover, and cook for 90 to 120 minutes, stirring occasionally, until the meat is very tender and pulling away from the bone. For the last 20 minutes, remove the lid and increase the heat slightly to reduce the sauce to a thick, coating consistency. Taste for salt before serving.

items.fix: Continue cooking for another 30 minutes. The collagen takes time. A probe thermometer at 90°C in the thickest piece indicates the collagen has broken down.
items.cause: Lamb neck cut too large; heat too low

items.fix: Toasting the curry in the oil for 30 seconds before adding other ingredients is the critical step for depth. The slow braise also develops flavour over time, at 1 hour the sauce tastes raw; at 2 hours it comes together.
items.cause: Curry powder not properly toasted; not enough time
items.symptom: Sauce tastes raw and spicy rather than deep

items.fix: Skim the surface fat once or twice during cooking with a spoon. Alternatively, make the dish a day ahead, refrigerate, and lift off the solid fat before reheating.
items.cause: Lamb neck is fatty; not skimmed during cooking
items.symptom: Too much fat in the sauce

items.fix: Remove the lid and simmer on medium heat for 20 minutes to reduce. The sauce should be thick enough to coat the meat.
items.cause: Too much water; lid kept on throughout
items.symptom: Sauce watery and thin at the end

content.text: Curry goat in Trinidad is party food and Sunday food: a pot that takes most of the day to prepare, eaten with dhalpuri roti and rice. It is cooked at Hindu religious celebrations like Divali and at secular parties and weddings. The goat is preferred over lamb for its stronger, more complex flavour; the long cooking transforms the tougher cuts into something very tender and deeply savoury. The Trinidadian version served wrapped in dhalpuri, sauce soaking into the roti, is one of the great street-food experiences of the Caribbean.
